issues.name: Enforceability of the father's oral promise to pay son's student loans
issues.support: The father promised to pay the son's student loans if the son married the politician's daughter within 18 months, but no written contract exists and the father refuses to pay.

sub_issues.name: Whether a valid contract was formed
sub_issues.support: Determining if there was offer, acceptance, and consideration or if the promise was a conditional gift.

sub_issues.name: Whether the Statute of Frauds applies to bar enforcement
sub_issues.support: The promise involves marriage consideration and possibly suretyship, both of which may require a writing.

sub_issues.name: Whether any exceptions to the Statute of Frauds apply
sub_issues.support: Consideration of promissory estoppel and main purpose doctrine.

sub_issues.name: Effect of the father's unilateral mistake about tax deduction
sub_issues.support: The father made the promise motivated by an expected tax deduction that turned out not to exist.

rules.name: Contract formation requires offer, acceptance, and consideration
rules.support: A promise conditioned on performance (marriage) can form a unilateral contract if the requested act is bargained-for consideration.

rules.name: Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing
rules.support: Contracts made upon consideration of marriage, other than mutual promises to marry, must be in signed writing to be enforceable.

rules.name: One-year provision of Statute of Frauds applies only if performance impossible within one year
rules.support: If contract can be performed within one year, writing is not required under this provision.

rules.name: Suretyship provision requires writing for promises to answer for another's debt
rules.support: Promises made to creditor to pay another's debt require writing unless exceptions apply.

rules.name: Main purpose (leading object) exception to suretyship writing requirement
rules.support: If promisor's main purpose is to secure personal economic benefit, writing may not be required.

rules.name: Promissory estoppel may enforce oral promises to avoid injustice
rules.support: Estoppel requires inducement and reliance but is often limited against Statute of Frauds categories.

rules.name: Unilateral mistake about expected benefit does not excuse performance
rules.support: Mistake about tax deduction does not excuse performance absent express condition.

elements.name: Offer
elements.parent_rule: Contract formation requires offer, acceptance, and consideration
elements.support: Father's conditional promise to pay loans if son marries politician's daughter within 18 months.

elements.name: Acceptance by performance
elements.parent_rule: Contract formation requires offer, acceptance, and consideration
elements.support: Son's marriage to politician's daughter within the specified period.

elements.name: Consideration (bargained-for exchange)
elements.parent_rule: Contract formation requires offer, acceptance, and consideration
elements.support: Whether son's marriage was induced by father's promise or was preexisting intent.

elements.name: Writing requirement for contracts in consideration of marriage
elements.parent_rule: Statute of Frauds requires certain contracts to be in writing
elements.support: Promise conditioned on son's marriage to specific person within 18 months.

elements.name: Performance within one year
elements.parent_rule: One-year provision of Statute of Frauds applies only if performance impossible within one year
elements.support: Son could have married within one year, actual marriage at 14 months.

elements.name: Promise to answer for another's debt
elements.parent_rule: Suretyship provision requires writing for promises to answer for another's debt
elements.support: Father's promise to pay son's existing student loans.

elements.name: Main purpose of promisor
elements.parent_rule: Main purpose (leading object) exception to suretyship writing requirement
elements.support: Father's motivation to obtain tax deduction.

elements.name: Inducement and reliance
elements.parent_rule: Promissory estoppel may enforce oral promises to avoid injustice
elements.support: Son's prior intent to marry weakens inducement.

factors.name: Father expressly conditioned promise on marriage within set time
factors.support: Shows requested performance and possible bargain.

factors.name: Son already planned to propose before promise
factors.support: Suggests promise may not have induced marriage.

factors.name: Promise made to son (debtor), not lender (creditor)
factors.parent_rule: Promise to answer for another's debt
factors.support: Weakens suretyship Statute of Frauds application.

factors.name: No signed writing exists
factors.parent_rule: Writing requirement for contracts in consideration of marriage
factors.support: Statute of Frauds bars enforcement without writing.

exceptions.name: Main purpose (leading object) exception to suretyship writing requirement
exceptions.support: If promisor's dominant purpose is personal economic benefit, writing may not be required.

exceptions.name: Promissory estoppel
exceptions.support: May enforce oral promise to avoid injustice if inducement and reliance exist, but limited against Statute of Frauds.

applications.name: Offer and acceptance formed a unilateral contract
applications.support: Father's conditional promise and son's marriage within 18 months.

applications.name: Consideration is questionable due to son's prior intent
applications.support: Son already planned to propose, weakening inducement argument.

applications.name: Statute of Frauds bars enforcement due to marriage consideration
applications.support: Promise conditioned on marriage to specific person, no signed writing.

applications.name: One-year provision does not apply
applications.support: Marriage could have occurred within one year.

applications.name: Suretyship provision likely does not apply
applications.support: Promise made to son, not creditor; no writing required.

applications.name: Main purpose exception does not overcome marriage-consideration writing requirement
applications.support: Even if main purpose applies, separate marriage-consideration SoF applies.

applications.name: Promissory estoppel unlikely to enforce promise
applications.support: Weak inducement and reliance; courts cautious to override SoF.

applications.name: Father's unilateral mistake about tax deduction irrelevant
applications.support: Mistake does not excuse performance absent express condition.

counterarguments.name: Son's prior intent to marry negates inducement
counterarguments.support: Son agreed because he already planned to propose, so promise did not induce marriage.

counterarguments.name: Promissory estoppel could enforce promise despite SoF
counterarguments.support: Son might argue injustice without enforcement, but reliance is weak and courts cautious.

counterarguments.name: Father's mistake about tax deduction excuses performance
counterarguments.support: Father believed in tax benefit, but mistake generally does not excuse unless condition stated.

conclusions.name: Father's oral promise is unenforceable
conclusions.support: Promise falls within marriage-consideration Statute of Frauds requiring writing, which is absent.

conclusions.name: One-year Statute of Frauds provision does not apply
Suretyship and main purpose issues do not overcome marriage-consideration writing requirement
conclusions.support: Separate and independent SoF category controls.

conclusions.name: Promissory estoppel is unlikely to enforce promise
conclusions.support: Weak reliance and inducement, plus SoF caution.

conclusions.name: Father's tax deduction mistake does not excuse performance
conclusions.support: Mistake is unilateral and no express condition on deduction.
